general: The protocol's cover sheet must include the review title, reviewer names, lead reviewer contact, and sources of support.
Include a timetable with target dates for key tasks such as searches, data extraction, and report preparation.
Specify plans for updating the review, including who will be responsible and the expected frequency.
Acknowledge all individuals contributing to the protocol preparation who are not listed on the cover sheet.
Report any real or perceived conflict of interest, including personal, political, academic, and financial, and avoid direct funding from single vested sources.
Include statements about potential conflicts in the protocol, even if confident judgments will not be influenced.
Ensure all cited works have complete references.
Place any tables and figures included in the protocol after the text.
Adhere to the predetermined protocol, but document necessary changes, ensuring they are not made based on how they affect review results.
Avoid post hoc decisions, such as excluding selected studies, when their impact on results is known, due to high bias susceptibility.
Work on the manuscript regularly to maintain familiarity and motivation.
Begin drafting the manuscript, especially the Introduction and Methods, even before the study is completed to identify and address missing aspects.
Construct a theoretical draft of the manuscript in a distraction-free environment within 1-2 hours before starting the actual writing.
Promptly insert references while writing the manuscript, preferably using reference management software.
Select a target journal by ensuring the manuscript offers innovative messages or new perspectives and aligns with the journal's agenda.
Seek pre-peer review from internal (general and expert) and external referees before submission, contacting them beforehand to confirm availability.
Critically review the manuscript at least three times after completion: first for logic and organization, second for syntax and grammar, and third for objectivity after a 1-2 week break.
Ensure the manuscript has a single, clear main message, avoiding the inclusion of multiple unrelated ideas or results.
Constantly ask "What is the objective of the study?" at every stage of the writing process to maintain focus.
Critically evaluate reviewer comments without assuming a defensive attitude, and re-review the literature while awaiting feedback.
Read the journal’s guidelines for discussion and conclusion sections before writing to ensure you meet their expectations.
Develop writing as a skill through systematic instruction and consistent practice.
Adopt concrete behaviors for productive writing, such as making a schedule, setting clear goals, tracking work, and building good habits.
Prioritize scientific communication and avoid publishing "fluffy nonsense" solely to increase publication count.
Allot specific, regular times for writing in your weekly planner instead of attempting to "find" time.
Start by scheduling a minimum of 4 hours per week for writing, focusing on regularity over the number of hours.
Ruthlessly defend your scheduled writing time by avoiding distractions like email, phone, meetings, or other tasks.
Treat your scheduled writing time with the same professional commitment as your teaching time.
Use your allotted writing time for all instrumental tasks, including data analysis, reading articles, outlining, or reviewing submission guidelines.
Do not let a lack of ideal equipment (e.g., a new computer, printer) or a dedicated writing space serve as a barrier to productivity.
While "windfall writing" outside scheduled times is beneficial, never allow it to replace your committed writing schedule.
Begin drafting sections like Introduction and Methods before study completion to identify and address potential issues early.
Promptly place references using software programs, avoiding last-minute sorting.
Conduct a pre-peer review with internal and external referees to gather feedback before submission.
Critically review and refine the manuscript based on reviewer comments, maintaining an objective, non-defensive stance.
Re-review the literature while awaiting reviewer feedback, as new relevant articles may have been published.
Ensure the manuscript conveys a single, clear main message, avoiding multiple unrelated ideas or results.
Consistently ask 'What is the objective of the study?' at every writing stage to maintain focus.
Review the finalized manuscript at least three times: first for logic and organization, second for syntax and grammar, and thirdly after 1-2 weeks for objective assessment.
Avoid publishing rushed, low-quality studies that could damage your scientific reputation.
Assess if your study's depth and breadth align with articles in your target journal to determine if it constitutes a single paper.
Consider writing multiple manuscripts if your study yields two or more distinct central results that can stand alone.
Provide citations for factual statements, surprising findings, and non-obvious suggestions, but not for universally obvious facts.
Always include citations when referring to previous work, discoveries, or ideas.
Prioritize citing the first robust demonstration of a discovery to credit original work and trace the path of knowledge.
Include citations that represent diverse views or conflicting results to offer a balanced appraisal of the topic.
Avoid citation bias by ensuring you credit meritorious work fairly, regardless of article characteristics or author identity.
Cite extensively during drafting, then judiciously remove less necessary references to meet journal limits while maintaining balance.
Ensure your writing demonstrates consistent expertise and sufficient depth in key areas to guide a diverse readership effectively.
Begin writing without waiting to feel fully immersed in the literature or to have definitively found your place within it.
Actively use the writing process to deepen your understanding of existing work and establish your own position in the field.
Analyze your target journals to understand their specific genre, prevailing conventions, and values.
Cultivate persistence in your writing practice, as it is a key characteristic of productive academic writers.
Consistently apply writing strategies and commit to them for a sufficient period to observe their benefits.
Be flexible with your writing habits, incorporating both short, regular "snack" sessions (e.g., 30 minutes daily) and longer, intensive "binge" periods.
Prioritize your writing over external distractions like rivalry or subjective criticism.
Set specific, achievable goals and targets for your writing to monitor progress and maintain motivation.
Invite trusted colleagues and friends to provide comments on your early drafts.
Do not rely on journal editors and and reviewers for basic writing guidance; actively seek formal or informal writing development instead.
Limit the number of distinct ideas presented in a single paper.
Avoid overstating the problem, making excessive claims for your solution, or over-critiquing others' work; instead, modulate your tone and prune your paper.
Create a flexible, rough plan for your writing, but be willing to adapt it as you write.
Revise and redraft your paper at least twice before submission.
Allocate dedicated time for writing each week, even if it's just 2-5 hours during term time.
Set clear goals and targets for your writing projects to maintain focus.
Solicit feedback on early drafts from trusted colleagues and friends.
Practice regular writing in short, consistent bursts (e.g., 30 minutes daily) alongside any longer "binge" sessions.
Utilize freewriting as a "low stakes" technique to develop ideas and confidence without immediate self-censorship.
Address any personal ambivalence or negative feelings towards academic journals in your field, as these can hinder your writing progress.
Choose a suitable structural design for your writing and adhere to it.
Plan your writing deliberately by foreseeing the overall shape of your composition.
Make the paragraph the fundamental unit of composition.
Divide your subject into topics, with each topic dealt with in its own paragraph to aid the reader.
Begin each paragraph with a topic sentence or a sentence that facilitates transition.
Break long paragraphs into shorter ones to improve readability, even if not strictly necessary for logical development.
Revise and rewrite your work to improve its quality.
Place yourself (the author) in the background, focusing on the subject matter.
Do not inject personal opinion into scientific writing.
Always adhere to specific instructions provided by your lecturer or tutor regarding abstract content.
Always adhere to specific guidelines provided by the course instructor or academic journal, as these take precedence over general advice.
Adhere to the target journal's instructions regarding style, word count, and formatting for the title and abstract.
Include important keywords in both the title and abstract for effective indexing and retrieval by search engines.
List keywords after the abstract.
Browse titles and abstracts from prominent journals, especially your target journal, to learn about effective wording and styles.
Avoid grammatical and typographic errors in the abstract, as they reflect carelessness and poor quality.
title: The title should reflect the most striking or newest outcome among the results obtained.
Determine 2-3 potential titles before writing the manuscript to boost motivation.
Select the final title based on the intensity of the discussion and its suitability for the target journal's agenda.
Formulate 2-3 potential titles before writing to boost motivation.
Investigate the suitability of the chosen title for the target journal's agenda beforehand.
The title should highlight the most striking or newest outcome of the study.
Craft your title to include key terms that facilitate indexing by search engines and bibliographic databases, enhancing discoverability.
Ensure the title is descriptive, direct, accurate, appropriate, interesting, concise, precise, unique, and not misleading.
Craft the title to provide just enough detail to pique reader interest, encouraging further reading of the abstract and full paper.
Place important keywords at the beginning of the title to maximize visibility in search engines.
Avoid whimsical, amusing, or clever titles, as they may be overlooked or taken less seriously.
Avoid excessively long or complicated titles, which can deter readers.
Draft the title after the main body of the text and the abstract are complete to ensure accuracy.
Prefer descriptive or neutral titles that present essential elements without revealing the main result or conclusion.
Avoid declarative titles (stating the main finding) and interrogative titles (posing a question) for research articles.
Incorporate the PICO (Patients, Interventions, Comparisons, and Outcome) elements into the title.
Avoid nonstandard abbreviations, acronyms, technical jargon, chemical formulas, and numerical values (unless essential) in the title.
Seek an impartial colleague's opinion before finalizing the title.
If required, include the study design in the title, often separated by a colon.
abstract: Write the Abstract section after the completion of the main manuscript.
If an abstract was previously presented (e.g., at a congress), use it as a guide to prevent deviation from the main objective during writing.
If an abstract was previously presented, use it as a guide to ensure the manuscript stays focused on its main objective.
Write the abstract after completing the main body of the paper to ensure a clear overview of findings and conclusions.
Begin by re-reading your entire paper and condensing the key information from each section into one to two sentences.
Review these condensed sentences to confirm they cover all major points and adhere to the specified word count.
Edit the abstract for smooth flow and clear expression.
Structure the abstract as a single, well-developed paragraph that is coherent, concise, and self-contained.
Ensure the abstract covers all essential academic elements of the full paper, such as background, purpose, methods, results, and conclusions.
Do not include any information in the abstract that is not present in the main body of the paper, and avoid including references.
For descriptive abstracts, include background, purpose, and focus, but omit methods, results, and conclusions if it's a research paper.
For informative abstracts, briefly summarize background, purpose, focus, methods, results, and conclusions, dedicating one to two sentences to each part.
Write the abstract only after completing the full paper to ensure an accurate summary.
Keep the abstract concise, typically 6-7 sentences or 150-250 words, forming a single paragraph.
Ensure the abstract is self-contained and fully comprehensible without needing to read the entire paper.
Avoid citing references within the abstract; focus solely on your own research.
Provide the context, background, and specific topic of your research, along with the central questions or problem addressed.
Summarize what is already known about the question or previous research findings.
Explain the rationale and goals for your research, such as filling a gap or applying new methods.
Describe your research and/or analytical methods concisely.
Present your main findings, results, or arguments.
Outline the significance or implications of your findings or arguments.
Ensure the abstract is simple, specific, clear, unbiased, honest, concise, precise, stand-alone, complete, scholarly, and not misleading.
Maintain consistency between the abstract and the main text, especially after revisions, ensuring the key message is prominent.
Draft the abstract after the main text is complete to ensure accuracy and alignment with the paper's content.
Adhere strictly to the target journal's specific instructions for abstract format, word limit (typically 200-300 words), and subheadings.
Utilize the full word capacity allowed by the journal to represent the most important scientific facts.
For structured abstracts, use specific subheadings (e.g., Background, Objectives, Methods, Results, Conclusions) as required by the journal.
In the 'Background/Objectives' section, state the rationale for the work, hypothesis, and major objectives concisely.
In the 'Methods' section, provide essential details of the study design, setting, participants, interventions, outcome measures, and analysis.
In the 'Results' section, present important findings, including participant numbers, analysis results, and actual data (e.g., P values, confidence intervals).
In the 'Conclusions' section, state the take-home message and significant findings, interpreting results without overinterpretation, and include study implications.
Avoid nonstandard abbreviations, references, tables, or figures within the abstract.
Seek an impartial colleague's opinion on the abstract's content before finalization.
If applicable, publish the clinical trial registration number at the end of the abstract.
introduction: Start the background section by setting out the rationale for the review and explaining the importance of the questions asked.
Include a conceptual discussion of the research problem and a statement of its significance.
Present a brief overview of the research question, covering its theoretical, practical, and methodological history.
Address qualitative and historical debates surrounding the research question.
Discuss previous reviews of the research topic, highlighting lessons learned, inconsistencies, and methodological strengths/weaknesses.
Emphasize the contribution of the new review by clearly stating the unresolved empirical questions and controversies it addresses.
Formulate objectives that help people make practical decisions about social and behavioural interventions and public policy.
Ensure objectives address the practical choices people face regarding policy or practice adoption.
Address outcomes in the objectives that are meaningful to public policy decision-makers.
Draft the Introduction section early in the writing process, even before the study is fully completed.
Avoid spending too much time or space on the research 'problem' in early drafts, as this often leads to an over-long text.
Do not overstate the significance of the problem or make excessive claims for your proposed solutions.
Be careful not to overstate your critique of others' work when establishing the knowledge gap.
Include background, purpose, a clear proposition (thesis statement), and an outline of key issues in your Introduction.
Use the Introduction to introduce the paper and foreground the issues that will be discussed.
methods: Clearly state the criteria for inclusion and exclusion of studies, specifying characteristics that determine relevance and providing examples.
Present a detailed search strategy, including sources, years covered, keywords, and mechanisms for retrieving unpublished documents, with a rationale for choices.
Adopt an international perspective for literature searching, avoiding restrictions by nationality, samples, or language unless justified.
Describe methodologies common in the primary research covered, focusing on participant sampling, research design, and measurement techniques, using exemplary studies.
Explain how instances of multiple outcome measures from a single evaluation will be handled, carefully spelling out criteria for determining independent data points.
Describe the characteristics of included studies that will be retrieved as potential moderators of outcomes, specify study outcomes of interest, and detail how coding reliability will be established.
Describe the statistical procedures and conventions for quantitative analysis, including software, effect size metrics, handling missing data, combining results, and assessing variability.
Provide a rationale for each statistical procedural choice and convention, considering its potential impact on review outcomes.

For qualitative research, operationally describe all methodological aspects (inclusion/exclusion, primary methods, independent findings, characteristics) with the same detail as for quantitative research.
Draft the Methods section early in the writing process, even before the study is fully completed.
results: Construct tables containing numerical data early in the writing process for inclusion in the Results section.
Do not present results that lack supportive evidence or are equivocal.
Avoid repeating numerical values from the Results section in the Discussion unless absolutely necessary.
Construct tables containing numerical data early, even before study completion, for inclusion in the Results section.
discussion: Structure the Discussion section into three parts: an introductory paragraph, intermediate paragraphs, and a concluding paragraph.
Limit the Discussion section to 6-7 paragraphs, with each paragraph not exceeding 200 words.
For intermediate paragraphs, use a "divide and conquer" approach, dedicating a full paragraph to each study finding, ordered by importance.

Begin each intermediate paragraph with an "indisputable" introductory sentence that answers "What have we found?", then discuss your findings using current literature, revealing new ideas, and ending with a concluding remark.
In intermediate paragraphs, present supporting studies in a logical sequence (e.g., past to present, few to many cases) and highlight aspects that contradict other studies.
Address potential flaws, reasons for those flaws (e.g., inadequate blinding, low statistical power), and the meaning of your outcomes within the relevant paragraphs.

The introductory paragraph should analyze the problem and its updateness without repeating the Introduction, starting with an undebatable sentence and addressing specific questions about the study's contribution.
The concluding paragraph should mention the study's strong points using constrained statements, indicate limitations, and emphasize future directions or potential clinical applications.
Avoid mentioning all literature references; focus only on results relevant to the specific paragraph, as the Discussion is not a review article.
Cite original articles when referring to previous studies, avoiding abstracts or review articles unless absolutely necessary.
Structure the discussion across two or three paragraphs for a comprehensive understanding of the study.
Begin with a clear statement of the principal findings.
Include your research results, discuss related research, and compare your findings with your initial hypothesis.
Explain the importance and implications of your study's outcomes realistically, based on previous literature.
State whether results prove or disprove your hypothesis, and if disproved, suggest potential reasons.
Introduce new ways to think about the research question and indicate next steps for future research.
Present limitations and negative results to give a complete understanding, but do not apologize for them or undermine your authority.
Avoid rewriting your abstract or using phrases like "we investigated" or "we studied."
Do not introduce new arguments or evidence not previously discussed in the main body of the paper.
Do not overstate the importance of your findings or make grand claims about resolving large questions.
Limit the Discussion section to 6-7 paragraphs, not exceeding the combined length of the Introduction, Methods, and Results sections, with each paragraph under 200 words.
Structure the Discussion into an introductory paragraph, intermediate paragraphs, and a concluding paragraph.
The introductory paragraph should state the study's main idea, analyze the problem's updateness without repeating the Introduction, and summarize basic findings.
Start the introductory paragraph with an undebatable sentence, addressing the study's focus, solutions, innovation, and contribution.
For intermediate paragraphs, use a 'divide and conquer' approach, dedicating a paragraph to each study finding in order of importance.
Begin each intermediate paragraph with an 'indisputable' sentence stating your finding, then discuss it with current literature, new ideas, and a concluding remark.
In intermediate paragraphs, emphasize the main topic concisely, indicating its importance and presenting other studies logically, highlighting contradictions.
Critically evaluate results in intermediate paragraphs by questioning their validity, reasons for potential inadequacy, and their conveyed meaning.
Avoid discussing unsupported or equivocal results, and do not repeat numerical values from the Results section unless essential.

In the concluding paragraph, state the study's strong points using constrained language, indicate limitations objectively, suggest future directions or clinical applications, and cite only relevant original articles.
conclusion: Provide a clear "take-home" message for the reader to conclude the manuscript.
Explain how your conclusions reshape or add to existing knowledge in the field, referencing previous research.
Articulate why your results are important or relevant to your audience, noting if they support or disprove prior studies.
Suggest how future research can build on your observations, outlining key experiments that should be done.
style: Use simple, clear, and effective language throughout the manuscript, as if conversing with a colleague.
Ensure each sentence conveys a single point and does not exceed 25-30 words.
Place previously mentioned or known information at the beginning of a sentence and new information at the end.
Avoid unnecessary words and prefer active voice over passive voice.
Do not hesitate to start sentences with "we" to promote active voice and clarity.
Remove any word whose removal does not change the meaning of the text.
Avoid "word salads" or writing with weak logical meaning that does not lead the reader to a clear conclusion.
Prefer concise phrasing, such as "white" instead of "white in color," "proven" instead of "definitely proven," and "to" instead of "in order to."
Use direct and active constructions, for example, "We measured serum CEA levels" instead of "Measurements were performed to evaluate the levels of CEA in the serum."
Avoid superfluousness, unnecessary repetitions, over-criticizing or over-praising other studies, and pompous literary language.
Be concise, avoiding unnecessary detail that can distract from the main findings.
Use clear and concise language to summarize key findings.
Strive to write as well as you can, as well-written papers and grant proposals stand out.
Structure sentences by placing known information at the beginning and new information at the end.
Eliminate unnecessary words from sentences; every word should contribute to the meaning.
Prefer active voice over passive voice (e.g., 'We measured serum CEA levels' instead of 'Measurements were performed to evaluate the levels of CEA in the serum').
Do not hesitate to begin sentences with 'we' to promote active voice.
Avoid 'word salads' or writing with weak logical meaning that does not lead to conclusions.
Use concise phrasing, avoiding redundant words (e.g., 'white color' instead of 'white in color', 'proven' instead of 'definitely proven', 'to' instead of 'in order to').
Avoid superfluousness and unnecessary repetitions in your writing.
Adhere to the scholarly mantra of accuracy, neutrality, clarity, and precision in your writing.
Portray and interpret your observations accurately and neutrally in scientific writing.
In original research articles, use inferences and opinions sparingly and signal them explicitly with phrases like 'This result suggests...' or 'We speculate...'.
Ensure your language is clear, unambiguously transmitting your intended message, and precise, focusing exactly on concepts to convey their significance.
Write directly and clearly, avoiding indirect language that can cause a loss of focus.
Limit the number of distinct ideas presented in a single article to maintain a clear focus.
Develop strong rhetorical knowledge to construct arguments effectively, rather than merely asserting points.
Always provide necessary definitions and justifications for your assertions.
Be prepared to modulate your tone, trim critique, and prune your article when rhetorical flaws are identified.
Understand and apply principles of paragraph structure, including the use of topic sentences, to strengthen your argument.
Write directly and clearly to avoid losing focus through indirect language.
Adhere to your discipline's conventions regarding the use of personal experience, personal voice, and first-person pronouns, as these vary significantly across fields.
Strive for an objective, focused, and neutral tone in your academic writing.
Maintain a conservative and hierarchical structure in your arguments, as is conventional in academic research.
Use the active voice as it is generally more direct, vigorous, and concise than the passive voice.
Put statements in positive form, making definite assertions and avoiding evasive or noncommittal language.
Prefer definite, specific, and concrete language over vague or abstract terms to engage the reader.
Omit needless words; ensure every word in a sentence and every sentence in a paragraph contributes to the meaning.
Revise out debilitating expressions such as "the fact that" and superfluous phrases like "who is" or "which was."
Express coordinate ideas in similar grammatical form (parallel construction) to enhance clarity and readability.
Keep related words together in a sentence to prevent confusion and ambiguity.
Do not overwrite or overstate your points.
Avoid the use of qualifiers that weaken your assertions.
Do not join independent clauses with only a comma; use a semicolon or separate them into distinct sentences.
Write in plain English to ensure the abstract is understandable to both a general and a discipline-specific audience.
Employ passive voice when reporting findings to emphasize the issues rather than the researchers.
Use the language of the original paper, simplifying it where necessary for a broader readership.
Use the past tense when referring to previous research studies or completed events.
Employ the present tense for general facts, current interpretations, and prevailing explanations.
Describe the methods, findings, arguments, and implications of your *current* research study using the present tense.
Use the present tense to introduce your study and to explain its significance.
Be concise, precise, and meticulous in writing.
Ensure the title and abstract are pleasant to read.
Use simple language and phrases in the abstract, rather than complex sentences.
Avoid passive voice in the abstract.
Remove redundant words and extensively edit the abstract to meet the exact word count.
